elements.sent-2411-001: This work investigates the co-existence of sensing and communication functionalities \textcolor{black {in a \gls{bs serving a communication user in the uplink and simultaneously detecting a radar target with the same frequency resources.}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: To address inter-functionality interference, we employ \gls{rs at the communication user and \gls{sic at the joint radar-communication receiver at the \gls{bs .}}}
elements.sent-2411-003: This approach is motivated by \gls{rs 's proven effectiveness in mitigating inter-user interference among communication users.}
elements.sent-2411-004: Building on the proposed system model based on \gls{rs , we derive inner bounds on performance in terms of ergodic data information rate for communication and ergodic radar estimation information rate for sensing.}
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, we present a closed-form solution for the optimal power split in \gls{rs that maximizes the communication user’s performance.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The bounds achieved with \gls{rs are compared to conventional methods, including spectral isolation and full spectral sharing with \gls{sic .}}
elements.sent-2411-007: We demonstrate that \gls{rs offers a superior performance trade-off between sensing and communication functionalities compared to traditional approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-008: Pertinently, while the original concept of RS deals only with digital signals, this work brings forward \gls{rs as a general method for including non-orthogonal access for sensing signals.}
elements.sent-2411-009: As a consequence, the work done in this paper provides a systematic and parametrized way to effectuate non-orthogonal sensing and communication waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-010: With growing similarities in radio resources, hardware platforms, and signal processing techniques between communication and sensing, their integration has attracted significant attention from academia and industry alike\cite{Masouros@ISaC,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This integration promises to enable myriads of use-cases for future wireless networks including (but not limited to) smart cities, remote sensing, Internet-of-things, and vehicle-to-everything connectivity\cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC .}
elements.sent-2411-012: With that being said, a critical challenge in realizing this potential lies in managing the detrimental interference between communication and sensing while achieving an optimal performance trade-off \cite{Masouros@ISaC, Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC, Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-013: To this end, previous works have explored either spectral isolation or spectral sharing with \gls{sic to address this issue of interference \cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: However, spectral isolation may result in low resource efficiency, while spectral sharing with \gls{sic is more suited to systems prioritizing sensing, as communication rates are constrained by sensing interference\cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Thus, overcoming this challenge calls for the development of more efficient interference management strategies to ensure the seamless co-existence of the two functionalities\cite{Masouros@ISaC,Bliss@InnerBound,Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Schematic_Radar_Comm.pdf} \caption{A basic joint sensing-communication setup where a \gls{bs} is serving a communication user in the uplink and simultaneously sensing a radar target.} \label{fig:Schematic}
elements.sent-2411-016: In \gls{rs , a communication user's message is split into multiple parts which are then transmitted using \gls{sc at the transmitter and decoded using \gls{sic at the receiver\cite{RSintro16bruno,Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Building on \gls{rs , the \gls{rsma scheme serves multiple communication users by adjusting both the message split and the power allocation to different parts of the users' messages\cite{RSintro16bruno,mao2017rate .}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Through this, \gls{rsma effectively manages inter-user interference by partially decoding the interference and partially treating interference as noise\cite{mao2017rate,Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Such an approach allows \gls{rsma to achieve optimal \gls{dof in the downlink, as well as the capacity region in the uplink without requiring time-sharing among users\cite{RSintro16bruno,Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: To this end, we consider a \gls{bs with a joint radar-communication receiver serving a communication user in the uplink and simultaneously sensing a radar target; see Fig.~\ref{fig:Schematic .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This joint receiver is capable of simultaneously estimating radar target parameters from the radar echoes and decoding the received communication signals\cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}
elements.sent-2411-022: We also assume that the radar system operates without any constraints on the maximum unambiguous range.
elements.sent-2411-023: On the other hand, the communication user is employing \gls{rs at its side to transmit information.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Building on this, we derive the sensing-communication co-existence performance bounds for \gls{rs .}
elements.sent-2411-025: For the radar target, the performance bound is measured in terms of ergodic radar estimation information rate, whereas for the communication user it is ergodic data information rate\cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Moreover, we utilize the derived performance bounds to obtain the optimal power split for \gls{rs that maximizes the data information rate of the communication user.}
elements.sent-2411-027: We show that by virtue of its versatile design principle, \gls{rs manages the interference between sensing and communication functionalities more efficiently, and allows to achieve a better performance trade-off between the two compared to conventional approaches of spectral isolation and spectral sharing with \gls{sic}\cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Moreover, this work takes forward the concept of \gls{rs beyond digital signals only and puts it as a general method for including non-orthogonal access for sensing signals.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Consequently, this paper provides a systematic and parametrized way to effectuate non-orthogonal sensing and communication waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-030: To begin with, we outline the key assumptions made in this work, which align with those presented in \cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-031: It is important to note that both our system model and most of the assumptions closely follow the framework established in \cite{Bliss@InnerBound , which investigates the performance bounds for conventional approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Moreover, for simplicity, we restrict our focus on range estimation as we are concerned with investigating and comparing performance bounds of different approaches\cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, the work done in this paper can be extended to other estimation parameters as well\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Subsequently, below, we list the key assumptions made in this work:.
elements.sent-2411-035: Only the time portion where the radar return and communication signals overlap is considered for analysis.
elements.sent-2411-036: Next, we delineate the signal model for the radar return of the target, and of the communication user.
elements.sent-2411-037: We consider a system operating in the complex-baseband, where the \gls{bs is serving a communication user in the uplink and simultaneously tracking a radar target.}
elements.sent-2411-038: To this end, the \gls{bs transmits a radar signal $r\left(t\right)$ with power $P_{r}$ and unit variance\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: For the radar return signal, we denote the complex combined antenna gain, radar cross-section, and propagation gain by $a_{r}$, and time-delay by $\tau_{r}$.
elements.sent-2411-040: Subsequently, the two parts are independently encoded into streams of unit variance, $s_{c,1}\left(t\right)$ and $s_{c,2}\left(t\right)$.
elements.sent-2411-041: These streams are allocated powers $P_{c,1}$ and $P_{c,2}$ respectively, such that $P_{c,1 +P_{c,2}\leq P_{c}$, with $P_{c}$ as the total uplink transmit power available at the communication user.}
elements.sent-2411-042: While we consider one communication user here, the work done in this paper can be extended to multiple users case.
elements.sent-2411-043: Subsequently, the uplink transmit signal of the communication user, ${x}\left(t\right)$, is expressed as\cite{Mishra@tutorialRSMA,RSintro16bruno \begin{equation}\label{eq:comm_tx} x\left(t\right)=\sqrt{P_{c,1}}\,{s}_{c,1}\left(t\right) + \sqrt{P_{c,2}}\,{s}_{c,2}\left(t\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The complex combined antenna gain and propagation loss (amplitude) for $x\left(t\right)$ is denoted by $b_{c}$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Thereafter, at the \gls{bs , the overall signal received by the joint radar-communication receiver is passed through a brick-wall filter matched to the bandwidth $B$ of the system\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: This joint radar-communications complex-baseband received signal, denoted by $y\left(t\right)$, can be expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:joint_rx_signal} y\left(t\right)=b_{c}{x}\left(t\right) + \sqrt{P_{r}}\,a_{r}{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)+n\left(t\right),\end{equation} where ${n}\left(t\right)$ is the thermal noise which is drawn from a Gaussian distribution of zero mean and variance $\sigma_{n ^{2 =\kappa_{B T_{temp B$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Here, $\kappa_{B}$ and $T_{temp}$ denote the Boltzman constant and effective temperature, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-048: At the \gls{bs , the joint radar-communication receiver first decodes one stream of the communication user, then the radar return signal, and finally the other stream.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Without loss of generality, we assume the decoding order to be ${s _{c,1}\left(t\right)\rightarrow {r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right) \rightarrow {s _{c,2}\left(t\right)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In the following, we delineate the decoding process at the \gls{bs .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{RS_For_ISaC_Framework.pdf} \caption{Joint radar-communications system block diagram with \gls{rs} at the communication user and \gls{sic} at the \gls{bs}.} \label{fig:RS_SIC_Framework}
elements.sent-2411-051: It is worth noting that in analog-to-digital conversion at baseband, sampling requirements differ for radar and communication signals due to radar’s short pulse duration and need for high range resolution.
elements.sent-2411-052: Radar requires a high sampling rate to accurately capture temporal features for precise range measurements, whereas communication signals prioritize data fidelity over longer durations.
elements.sent-2411-053: These operations and their corresponding sampling considerations take place in the respective blocks of Fig.~\ref{fig:RS_SIC_Framework .}
elements.subsection-2411-002: First Communication Stream With Predicted Radar Return Suppressed
elements.sent-2411-054: As mentioned in Section \ref{Sysmod , since we assume the target is being tracked, we have prior knowledge of its range from previous observations, albeit with fluctuations due to an underlying random process associated with the target\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This range fluctuation can be translated into time-delay fluctuation which is modeled by a Gaussian distribution $n_{proc}$\cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Subsequently, during the $k^{th}$ observation, the delay for the target, $\tau_{r ^{\left(k\right)}$, can be written as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \tau_{r}^{\left(k\right)} =\tau_{r,pre}^{\left(k\right)}+n_{\tau_{r},proc} \tau_{r,pre}^{\left(k\right)} = f\left(k;T_{pri},\phi\right), \end{split}\end{equation} where $\tau_{r,pre}$ is the predicted time-delay, and $f\left(k;T_{pri ,\phi\right)$ is a prediction function for it.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The prediction function depends on the pulse repetition interval $T_{pri}$, and a set of system and target parameters, $\phi$\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The variance of the time-delay function process, $\sigma_{\tau_{r ,proc ^{2}$, is given by \begin{equation}\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}=\mathbb{E}\left\{\abs{n_{\tau_{r},proc}}^2\right\}=\mathbb{E}\left\{ \abs{\tau_{r}^{\left(k\right)}-f\left(k;T_{pri},\phi\right)} ^{2}\right\}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Using the predicted time-delay, the receiver seeks to mitigate interference from the radar return signal while decoding the first communication stream, $s_{c,1}$, by subtracting the predicted radar return from the received signal, $y\left(t\right)$\cite{Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC,Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-060: Consequently, the received signal with the predicted radar return suppressed can be written as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Tilde{y}\left(t\right)= \, b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,1}}\,{s}_{c,1}\left(t\right) + b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,2}}\,{s}_{c,2}\left(t\right)+ \sqrt{P_{r}}\,a_{r}\left[{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)-{r}\left(t-\tau_{r,pre}\right)\right]+n\left(t\right), \end{split}\end{equation} Note that the estimated amplitude is assumed to be equal to the actual amplitude, which is only reasonable to assume if the error in the time-delay is smaller than $1/B$\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-061: Further, the difference between the actual radar return and predicted radar return can be approximated by a derivative for small fluctuations as \begin{equation}{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)-{r}\left(t-\tau_{r,pre}\right) \approx \frac{\partial \,{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)}{\partial t}\,n_{\tau_{r},proc}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: Subsequently, $\Tilde{y}\left(t\right)$ can be approximated as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Tilde{y}\left(t\right)\approx \,b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,1}}\,{s}_{c,1}\left(t\right) + b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,2}}\,{s}_{c,2}\left(t\right) +\sqrt{P_{r}}\,a_{r}\frac{\partial \,{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)}{\partial t}\,n_{\tau_{r},proc}+n\left(t\right). \end{split}\end{equation} Following this, the interference plus noise from the first communications stream's point of view is given by \begin{equation}\begin{split} n_{int+n,1}\approx \,b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,2}}\,{s}_{c,2}\left(t\right)+ \sqrt{P_{r}}\,a_{r}\frac{\partial \,{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)}{\partial t}\,n_{\tau_{r},proc}+n\left(t\right), \end{split}\end{equation} and its power is given by\cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Mishra@tutorialRSMA \begin{equation}\sigma_{int+n,1}^{2}= \abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c,2} + P_{r}\abs{a_{r} }^{2}\left(2\,\pi\right)^{2}B_{rms}^{2}\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2} +\sigma_{n}^{2},\end{equation} where $B_{rms}$ is the root-mean squared radar bandwidth.}
elements.sent-2411-063: For the calculation of $B_{rms}$, please refer to \cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Bliss_Govindasamy_2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-064: The relationship between the bandwidth $B$ and $B_{rms}$ is given by \cite{Bliss_Govindasamy_2013,Bliss@InnerBound \begin{equation}\gamma^{2}B^{2}= \left(2\,\pi\right)^{2}B_{rms}^{2},\end{equation} where the scaling factor $\gamma$ is dependent on the shape of the radar waveform's power spectral density\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}}
elements.sent-2411-065: The value of $\gamma$ for a flat spectral wave is $\gamma^{2 =\left(2\,\pi\right)^{2 /12$.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Subsequently, the \gls{bs decodes the communication stream $s_{c,1}$ with interference from the suppressed radar return and stream $s_{c,2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Thereafter, using \gls{sic , the \gls{bs removes $s_{c,1}$ to then decode the radar signal, and stream $s_{c,2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: As mentioned in Section \ref{Sysmod , the analysis is for the case when there is an overlap between the communication signal and the radar return signal, i.e., the radar signal and the communication signal are received simultaneously\cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC,Bliss@InnerBound .}}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Radar Return Signal With Second Communication Stream As Interference
elements.sent-2411-069: In this subsection, we derive the \gls{crlb for the the time-delay estimation of the radar signal.}
elements.sent-2411-070: To this end, we consider a simple matched filter or correlation receiver for time-delay estimation\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Assuming perfect \gls{sic of the first communication stream $s_{c,1}$; \textcolor{black {as considered in \cite{Bliss@InnerBound for deriving the inner bounds on performance of a joint sensing-communication system for spectral sharing with \gls{sic ; the received signal at the time-delay estimator is expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Radar_Sig} z\left(t\right)= a_{r}\sqrt{P_{r}}\,{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right) + b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,2}}\,{s}_{c,2}\left(t\right) + n\left(t\right).\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: It is important to note that ${s _{c,2}\left(t\right)$ and $\tau_{r}$ are independent of each other.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Therefore, the presence of $b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,2 }\,{s _{c,2}\left(t\right)$ adds an independent, deterministic constant interference within the overlap period, which effectively increases the total noise variance.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The \gls{crlb for the estimation of $\tau_{r}$ through the considered correlation receiver would reflect this increased noise variance.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Additionally, this configuration can be achieved by having the \gls{bs send instructions to the communication user, as outlined at the beginning of Section \ref{Sysmod .\footnote{\textcolor{black {The authors wish to emphasize that when the symbol length of the communication signal is comparable to $T$, biased estimate of the time-delay may arise, particularly in cases where the pulse shape of the communication signal deviates from a rectangular form.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Such scenarios will arise when the symbol length of the communication signal is significantly reduced, and should be investigated in future research.
elements.sent-2411-077: To derive inner bounds on the performance of the joint sensing-communication system using \gls{rs , we account for the worst-case interference introduced by the secondary communication stream to the radar signal, which is the peak power of the second communication stream in baseband.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Subsequently, we derive the \gls{crlb for time-delay estimation in the presence of the second communication stream and noise.}
elements.sent-2411-079: To this end, the probability density function of the received signal with $\theta=\tau_{r}$ as the parameter of interest is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:pdf_rt} p\left(z\left(t\right);\theta\right)=\frac{1}{\pi \sigma_{eff}^{2}}e^{-\frac{\abs{z\left(t\right)-a_{r}\sqrt{P_{r}}{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)}^{2}}{\sigma_{eff}^{2}}},\end{equation} where $\sigma_{eff ^{2 =\sigma_{n ^{2 +\abs{b_{c ^{2 P_{c,2}$ is the combined noise variance and interference from stream ${s _{c,2}\left(t\right)$.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: However, since we are interested in the ergodic performance of the radar estimation information rate bound, we consider the expected value of $\abs{s_{c,2 ^{2}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Moreover, the consideration allows us analytical tractability.
elements.sent-2411-082: Subsequently, using \eqref{eq:pdf_rt , we can compute the Fisher Information, which then allows us to derive the \gls{crlb for the estimate of ${\tau_{r }$ as \cite{richards2010principles,Bliss@InnerBound \begin{equation}\label{eq:var_est} \sigma_{\tau_{r},est}^{2}\geq\left(\frac{\sigma_{n}^{2} + \abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c,2}}{2\gamma^{2}B^{2}\left(TB\right)\abs{ a_{r}}^{2}P_{r}}\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: With the obtained \gls{crlb for time-delay estimation, we calculate the radar estimation information rate\cite{Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC,Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}
elements.sent-2411-084: This estimation information rate is the data information rate equivalent of the communication user, and constitutes the entropy of the parameter being estimated and the entropy associated with the uncertainty of the estimation \cite{bliss@REIR,Bliss@InnerBound , expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:R_est_Stan} R_{est}\leq \frac{\delta}{2T}\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}}{\sigma_{\tau_{r},est}^{2}}\right)\end{equation} where $\delta$ is the radar duty factor, such that $T_{pri =T/\delta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: Next, with the estimated time-delay, we suppress the radar signal from $z\left(t\right)$ and estimate the second communication stream.
elements.subsection-2411-004: Second Communication Stream With Estimated Radar Return Suppressed
elements.sent-2411-086: The received signal at the communication receiver with estimated radar return suppressed can be written as \begin{equation}\begin{split} \Tilde{y}\left(t\right)= b_{c}\sqrt{P_{c,2}}\,{s}_{c,2}\left(t\right)+ \sqrt{P_{r}}\,a_{r}\left[{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)-{r}\left(t-\tau_{r,est}\right)\right]+n\left(t\right), \end{split}\end{equation} where during any $k^{th}$ observation we now have $\tau_{r ^{\left(k\right) =\tau_{r,est ^{\left(k\right) +n_{\tau_{r ,est}$, similar to the predicted radar return.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, the lower bound on the variance of $n_{\tau_{r ,est}$ is obtained in \eqref{eq:var_est .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Subsequently, the interference plus noise for ${s _{c,2}$ is \begin{equation}\begin{split} n_{int+n,2}\approx \sqrt{P_{r}}\,a_{r}\frac{\partial \,{r}\left(t-\tau_{r}\right)}{\partial t}\,n_{\tau_{r},est}+n\left(t\right), \sigma_{int+n,2}^{2} = P_{r}\abs{a_{r}}^2\gamma^2 B^2 \sigma_{\tau_{r},est}^{2} +\sigma_{n}^{2} \end{split}\end{equation} Following this, the data information rate for the second communication stream can be calculated.}
elements.sent-2411-089: To this end, in the next section we calculate the performance bounds for both the radar target and the communication user.
elements.sent-2411-090: In this section, we delineate bounds on the performance of the joint sensing-communication system for the \gls{rs strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-091: As mentioned earlier, we consider ergodic radar estimation information rate for the radar target and ergodic data information rate for the communication user as performance measures.
elements.sent-2411-092: To this end, the bounds are derived assuming that the radar pulse duration $T$ is held constant\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-093: Subsequently, using equations \eqref{eq:var_est and \eqref{eq:R_est_Stan , the ergodic radar estimation information rate bound for the target is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:R_est_InnerBound} R_{est}^{RS} \leq \frac{\delta}{2T}\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{2\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^{2}\left(TB\right)\abs{a_{r}}^{2}P_{r}}{\sigma_{n}^2+\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c,2}}\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Note that the bound is reached if the estimator can reach the \gls{crlb bound.}
elements.sent-2411-095: Next, the bound on the data information rate of the communication user is given by $R_{c ^{RS}\leq R_{c,1 +R_{c,2}$, where $R_{c,1}$ and $R_{c,2}$ are the data rates of the split parts of the communication user's message, expressed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:RS_Comm_Rates} R_{c,1}= B\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c,1}}{\sigma_{n}^2+P_{r}\abs{a_{r}}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^{2}\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}+\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c,2}}\right)\nonumber R_{c,2}= B\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c,2}}{\sigma_{n}^2+P_{r}\abs{a_{r}}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^{2}\sigma_{\tau_{r},est}^{2}}\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The inner bounds on the performance of the communication user can be obtained by varying the power split between $P_{c,1}$ and $P_{c,2}$ as $P_{c,1 =(1-\alpha)P_{c}$ and $P_{c,2 =\alpha P_{c}$, where $\alpha \in [0,1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Next, utilizing \eqref{eq:R_est_InnerBound -\eqref{eq:RS_Comm_Rates , we calculate the optimal $\alpha$ for which the communication user achieves the maximum ergodic data information rate.}}
elements.sent-2411-098: The optimal power split, $\alpha_{max ^{RS}$, for which the communication rate is maximum can be derived as \begin{equation}\label{eq:Optimal_power_eq} \argmax_{\alpha \in [0,1]}\,R_{c,1}+R_{c,2}\end{equation} To obtain $\alpha_{max ^{RS}$, we differentiate the sum $R_{c,1 +R_{c,2}$ with respect to $\alpha$ and set the derivative equal to zero, leveraging the concavity of the sum over $\alpha \in [0,1]$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: Solving \eqref{eq:quadratic_alpha , the $\alpha$ for which communication user's rate is maximum is obtained as \begin{equation}\label{eq:opt_alpha} \alpha_{max}^{RS}= \frac{-\sigma_{n}^2+\abs{a_{r}}\gamma B \sigma_{proc}\sqrt{P_{r}TB}}{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Inner Bounds on Performance with Baseline Schemes
elements.sent-2411-101: For comparison, we also delineate the inner bounds on the performance of the joint sensing-communication system considered in this work for \gls{isa , and resource sharing with \gls{sic only.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: The readers are requested to refer to \cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC for a detailed discussion on the inner bounds of these approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-103: Following this, the performance inner bounds for \gls{isa are given by\cite{Bliss@InnerBound \begin{equation}R_{est}^{ISA} \leq \,\frac{\delta}{2T}\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{2\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}\gamma^{2}\left(1-\mu\right)^{2}B^{2}TB\abs{a_{r}}^{2}P_{r}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}}\right), \nonumber R_{c}^{ISA} \leq \,\mu B\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c}}{\mu \sigma_{n}^{2}}\right),\end{equation} where $\mu\in[0,1]$ partitions the total bandwidth $B$ into two sub-bands, one for the radar target, $B_{r =(1-\mu)B$, and the other for the communication user, $B_{c =\mu B$ \cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: With this approach, each functionality operates without interference from the other.
elements.sent-2411-105: Subsequently, the time-delay estimation is done without any interference from the communication functionality.
elements.sent-2411-106: Consequently, the inner bounds on the performance of the joint sensing-communication system with \gls{sic is given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:sic_innerbound} R_{est}^{SIC} \leq \frac{\delta}{2T}\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{2\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^2\left(TB\right)\abs{a_{r}}^{2}P_{r}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}}\right)\nonumber R_{c}^{SIC}\leq B\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}+P_{r}\abs{a_{r}}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^{2}\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-107: In the next section, we present the derived inner bounds on the performance of the joint sensing-communication system, comparing the outcomes achieved with rate splitting (RS), spectral isolation (ISA), and successive interference cancellation (SIC) under a specific example parameter set.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this section, we demonstrate the derived inner bounds on the performance of the joint sensing-communication system with \gls{rs .}
elements.sent-2411-109: To this end, the parameters considered are delineated in Table \ref{tab:Table_params}\cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}
elements.sent-2411-110: We assume that the signal of the communication user is received through an antenna sidelobe, leading to different radar and communications receive signal gains.

elements.sent-2411-141: In this paper, we provided a novel approach based on \gls{rs at the communication user and \gls{sic at the \gls{bs for producing joint sensing-communications performance inner bounds.}}}
elements.sent-2411-142: With ergodic radar estimation information rate as the performance measure for the radar target, and ergodic data information rate for the communication user, inner bounds on performance with \gls{rs are investigated and compared with that of baseline schemes; spectral isolation, and spectral sharing with \gls{sic .}}
elements.sent-2411-143: We demonstrated that \gls{rs efficiently manages the interference between the sensing and communication functionalities and achieved a larger inner bound than the baseline approaches up to a certain power split.}
elements.sent-2411-144: With the \gls{bs aware of and able to control radar and communication parameters and constraints, the operating region can be determined for the joint system depending on its requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-145: Additionally, we derive a closed-form expression for the optimal power split that maximizes the ergodic data information rate of the communication user.
elements.sent-2411-146: As a consequence, while the original concept of RS deals only with digital signals, this work puts forth \gls{rs as a general method for including non-orthogonal access for sensing signals.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Resultantly, we provided a systematic and parametrized way to effectuate non-orthogonal sensing and communication waveforms.
elements.sent-2411-148: The first is extending the current model to multiple users, which would require optimizing user grouping (including the radar target), power allocation and decoding order for \gls{rs , incorporating multi-antenna settings, etc. The second direction involves expanding the system model to case of comparable pulse duration of radar and symbol length of the communication user.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The authors are currently exploring both directions to evaluate \gls{rs performance for joint sensing-communication systems across various configurations.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcaptionsoff \fi \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
elements.sent-2411-150: Future wireless networks are envisioned to facilitate the seamless coexistence of communication and sensing functionalities, thereby enabling the much-touted \gls{isac paradigm.}
elements.sent-2411-151: A key challenge in \gls{isac is managing inter-functionality interference while maintaining a balanced performance trade-off.}
elements.sent-2411-152: In this work, we propose a \gls{rs -inspired approach to address this challenge in an uplink \gls{isac scenario, where a \gls{bs serves an uplink communication user while detecting a radar target.}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: We derive inner bounds on ergodic data information rate for communication user and the ergodic radar estimation information rate for sensing target.
elements.sent-2411-154: A closed-form solution is also derived for the optimal power split in \gls{rs that maximizes the communication user’s performance.}
elements.sent-2411-155: Compared to \gls{oma - and \gls{noma -inspired approaches, the proposed approach achieves a more favorable sensing-communication trade-off {by virtue of the decoding order flexibility introduced through splitting the communication message .}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Notably, this is the first work to employ a \gls{rs -inspired strategy as a general framework for non-orthogonal coexistence of sensing and communication, extending its applicability beyond traditional digital-only settings.}
elements.sent-2411-157: This \gls{isac paradigm promises to enable myriads of use-cases, including smart cities, remote sensing, \gls{iot , and \gls{v2x connectivity, among others\cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-158: In the existing literature, the former is addressed using multiple access-assisted schemes, which leverage classical multiple access strategies such as \gls{oma and \gls{noma to suppress inter-user interference within the communication domain\cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}}
elements.sent-2411-159: In multi-user \gls{isac scenarios, such suppression implicitly improves joint performance by enhancing the communication functionality \cite{NOMA_Assisted@Downlink,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}
elements.sent-2411-160: On the other hand, multiple access-inspired schemes explicitly target {inter-functionality interference , i.e., interference between the communication and sensing functionalities.}
elements.sent-2411-161: These schemes operate either via spectral isolation or through spectral sharing with \gls{sic , and are commonly referred to as \gls{oma -inspired and \gls{noma -inspired approaches, respectively\cite{Bliss@InnerBound,Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC,NOMA_Inspired@Downlink .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Notably, while multiple access-assisted schemes have been thoroughly investigated in the \gls{isac literature, research into multiple access-inspired schemes are in their early stages\cite{Yuanwei@Uplink_ISaC,Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}
elements.sent-2411-163: Bridging this gap is crucial to addressing interference challenges, enhancing resource efficiency, and enabling robust \gls{isac capabilities in future wireless networks\cite{Masouros@ISaC,Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}

elements.sent-2411-164: Specifically, the \gls{oma -inspired approach may suffer from low resource efficiency, while the \gls{noma -inspired approach may lead to one functionality being interference-limited by the other due to its fixed decoding order \cite{Yuanwei@NOMA_ISaC .}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: To overcome these limitations, the \gls{rs approach offers a promising alternative\cite{Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}
elements.sent-2411-166: Within the communication domain, \gls{rs has gained significant traction as a robust interference management technique\cite{RSintro16bruno,mishra2021ratesplitting,Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}
elements.sent-2411-167: In \gls{rs , a user's message is split into multiple parts, transmitted using \gls{sc at the transmitter and decoded via \gls{sic at the receiver\cite{RSintro16bruno .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-168: Building on \gls{rs , the \gls{rsma scheme caters to multi-user scenarios by optimizing message splitting and power allocation \cite{Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: This enables RSMA to manage inter-user interference in the communication domain effectively by partially decoding interference and partially treating it as noise, thereby outperforming conventional multiple access schemes\cite{Mishra@tutorialRSMA .}
elements.sent-2411-170: However, to the best of our knowledge, an \gls{rs -inspired approach specifically targeting inter-functionality interference in \gls{isac has not yet been investigated in the existing literature.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Interestingly, just as \gls{rsma generalizes \gls{noma for communication, the \gls{rs -inspired approach here generalizes the \gls{noma -inspired approach.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-203: Next, we obtain $p\left(\mathbf{z ;\theta=\tau_{r}\right)$ by marginalizing $p\left(\mathbf{z ;\theta,s_{c,2}\right)$ over $s_{c,2}$ with its distribution $p(s_{c,2 )$ as \cite{NConv@IN \begin{equation}\label{eq:Radar_Sig_Dist_SN_P} \begin{split} p\left(\mathbf{z};\theta=\tau_{r}\right) = \int p\left(\mathbf{z};\theta,s_{c,2}\right)\,p\left(s_{c,2}\right)ds_{c,2}, \end{split}\end{equation} which is equivalent to a convolution of two Gaussians, giving: \begin{equation}\label{eq:Radar_Sig_Dist_N} p\left(\mathbf{z};\theta\right) \sim \mathcal{CN}\left(a_{r}\sqrt{P_{r}}\,\mathbf{r}_{\tau_{r}}, \lvert{b_{c}}\rvert^{2}{P_{c,2}}\,\mathbf{h}\mathbf{h}^{H}+\sigma_{n}^{2}\mathbf{I}\right).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-204: Building on \eqref{eq:Radar_Sig_Dist_N , with mean $\boldsymbol{u _{z (\tau_{r )=a_{r}\sqrt{P_{r }\,\mathbf{r _{\tau_{r }$ and covariance matrix $\boldsymbol{\Sigma _{z =\lvert{b_{c }\rvert^{2 {P_{c,2 }\,\mathbf{h}\mathbf{h ^{H +\sigma_{n ^{2}\mathbf{I}$, we calculate the \gls{fim as \AM{\begin{subequations \begin{equation}J(\tau_{r}) = \left( \frac{\partial \mu_{z}(\tau_{r})}{\partial \tau_{r}} \right)^{H} \Sigma_{z}^{-1} \left( \frac{\partial \mu_{z}(\tau_{r})}{\partial \tau_{r}} \right),\label{eq:FIM_Expa} =\lvert{a_{r}}\rvert^{2}P_{r}(r_{\tau_{r}}^{'})^{H}(\lvert{b_{c}}\rvert^{2}{P_{c,2}}\,hh^{H}+\sigma_{n}^{2}I)^{-1}(r_{\tau_{r}}^{'}),\label{eq:FIM_Expb} =\frac{\lvert{a_{r}}\rvert^{2}P_{r}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}}\left(\lVert \smash{r_{\tau_{r}}^{'}} \rVert^2 - \frac{\varrho \lvert{h^{H}r_{\tau_{r}}^{'}}\rvert^{2}}{1 + \varrho \|h\|^2}\right),\label{eq:FIM_Expc} \geq\frac{{\lvert{a_{r}}\rvert^{2}P_{r}}\lVert \smash{r_{\tau_{r}}^{'}} \rVert^2}{\sigma_{n}^{2} + \lvert{b_{c}}\rvert^{2}{P_{c,2}}}\label{eq:FIM_Expd}\end{equation} \end{subequations where \eqref{eq:FIM_Expc uses the Sherman–Morrison formula with $\varrho = \lvert b_c \rvert^2 P_{c,2 / \sigma_n^2$, and \eqref{eq:FIM_Expd applies the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-224: Following this, the performance inner bounds for \gls{oma -inspired approach are given by\cite{Bliss@InnerBound \begin{equation}R_{est}^{\textrm{\gls{oma}}} \leq \,\frac{\delta}{2T}\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{2\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}\gamma^{2}\left(1-\mu\right)^{2}B^{2}TB\abs{a_{r}}^{2}P_{r}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}}\right), \nonumber R_{c}^{OMA} \leq \,\mu B\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c}}{\mu \sigma_{n}^{2}}\right),\end{equation} where $\mu\in[0,1]$ partitions the total bandwidth $B$ into two sub-bands, one for the radar target, $B_{r =(1-\mu)B$, and the other for the communication user, $B_{c =\mu B$ \cite{Bliss@InnerBound .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Subsequently, the time-delay estimation is done without any interference from the communication user.
elements.sent-2411-226: Consequently, the inner bounds for \gls{noma -inspired approach are given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:sic_innerbound} R_{est}^{\textrm{\gls{noma}}} \leq \frac{\delta}{2T}\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{2\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^2\left(TB\right)\abs{a_{r}}^{2}P_{r}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}}\right),\nonumber R_{c}^{\textrm{\gls{noma}}}\leq B\log_{2}\left(1+\frac{\abs{b_{c}}^{2}P_{c}}{\sigma_{n}^{2}+P_{r}\abs{a_{r}}^{2}\gamma^{2}B^{2}\sigma_{\tau_{r},proc}^{2}}\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-227: Together with the antenna gains, we calculate large scale coefficients in ${a_{r }$ using \cite[Eq. $(2.8)$]{richards2010principles , and ${b_{c }$ using the standard free-space path-loss model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-228: The results generalize to other cellular scenarios by proportionally scaling the parameters in Table \ref{tab:Table_params .}

elements.sent-2411-253: In this paper, we proposed a novel approach utilizing \gls{rs at the communication user and \gls{sic at the \gls{bs to establish joint sensing-communication performance inner bounds.}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Our results demonstrate that \gls{rs effectively mitigates inter-functionality interference and achieves a larger inner bound up to a certain power split, thereby allowing for an improved spectral efficiency of the system .}
elements.sent-2411-255: Additionally, we derived a closed-form expression for the optimal power split that maximizes the ergodic \gls{dir .}
elements.sent-2411-256: While traditional \gls{rs applies to digital signals, our work is the first work to extend it as a general framework for non-orthogonal sensing and communication waveforms.. \ifCLASSOPTIONcaptionsoff \fi \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
